walkthrough.title: Separate the Card Types
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Star Realms. First, separate all the cards into their types. Pull out the ten Explorer cards and set them in a face-up pile to the side. Then find each player's starting deck: eight Scouts and two Vipers per player. The remaining eighty cards form the Trade Deck.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Trade Deck
walkthrough.text: Take all eighty Trade Deck cards and give them a thorough shuffle. These cards contain ships and bases from four factions: Trade Federation in blue, Blob in green, Star Empire in yellow, and Machine Cult in red. Place the shuffled deck face down in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Trade Row
walkthrough.text: Flip the top five cards from the Trade Deck face up in a row next to the deck. This is the Trade Row, which is the market of cards available for purchase. Whenever a card is bought or scrapped from the Trade Row, immediately replace it with a new card from the Trade Deck.

walkthrough.title: Place the Explorer Pile
walkthrough.text: Set the ten Explorer cards in a face-up pile beside the Trade Row. Explorers are always available to purchase for two Trade, regardless of what is in the Trade Row. They are a reliable source of Trade generation and can be scrapped later for Combat when you no longer need them.

walkthrough.title: Build Starting Decks
walkthrough.text: Each player takes eight Scout cards and two Viper cards to form their ten-card starting deck. Scouts provide one Trade each, and Vipers provide one Combat each. Shuffle your starting deck well and place it face down in front of you. This is your personal draw pile.

walkthrough.title: Set Starting Authority
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with fifty Authority, which is your life total. Use the Authority cards to track this. Each player takes one twenty card and three ten cards, or any combination that equals fifty. Place these near your play area where both players can see them.

walkthrough.title: Draw Opening Hands
walkthrough.text: Both players draw three cards from their personal decks for their opening hand. This is a special first-turn rule. From the second turn onward, you will draw five cards at the end of each turn. The reduced first hand keeps the game balanced for both players. Choose a starting player randomly, and let's begin.

bullets: Pull out 10 Explorer cards → face-up pile to the side
Each player gets 8 Scouts + 2 Vipers (starting deck)
Remaining 80 cards = Trade Deck

bullets: Shuffle all 80 trade cards thoroughly

bullets: Flip 5 cards face up from Trade Deck

bullets: 10 Explorers face up beside the Trade Row
Always available for 2 Trade
Can be scrapped later for Combat

bullets: 8 Scouts (1 Trade each) + 2 Vipers (1 Combat each) per player
Shuffle → face-down personal draw pile

summary.title: Set Starting Authority
bullets: Each player starts at 50 Authority (life total)

summary.title: Draw Opening Hands
bullets: Both players draw 3 cards (first turn only)
Normal hand size = 5 cards from turn 2 onward
Choose starting player randomly

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Star Realms is a two-player deck-building card game. Each player starts with a weak ten-card deck and builds it by buying powerful ships and bases from a shared Trade Row. You use Trade to purchase new cards and Combat to attack your opponent's Authority. The first player to reduce their opponent to zero Authority wins.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: Your turn has three phases. First is the Main Phase, where you play cards, buy new ones, and attack. Second is the Discard Phase, where you discard all played ships and remaining hand cards to your discard pile. Third is the Draw Phase, where you draw five new cards. You can do things in any order during the Main Phase, which gives you a lot of flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Playing Cards
walkthrough.text: During the Main Phase, play cards from your hand into your play area. You can play as many cards as you want in any order. Each card generates Trade, Combat, or both. Trade and Combat pool together during your turn. You can interleave playing cards with buying and attacking. There is no limit to how many cards you can play.

walkthrough.title: Buying Cards
walkthrough.text: Use your pooled Trade to buy cards from the Trade Row or the Explorer pile. Pay a card's Trade cost to acquire it. The acquired card goes directly to your discard pile, not your hand. When you buy from the Trade Row, immediately flip a replacement card from the Trade Deck. You can buy multiple cards in a turn if you have enough Trade.

walkthrough.title: Attacking with Combat
walkthrough.text: Spend your pooled Combat to reduce your opponent's Authority. Any unspent Combat at the end of your turn is wasted, so always spend it. If your opponent has Outpost bases in play, you must destroy all Outposts before you can deal Combat damage to the player directly or attack non-Outpost bases.

walkthrough.title: Ships and Bases
walkthrough.text: Cards come in two types: ships and bases. Ships are played from your hand, provide their effect, and get discarded at end of turn. Bases are played to your play area and stay in front of you turn after turn until your opponent destroys them. Bases have a defense value that your opponent must spend Combat to destroy. This makes bases incredibly valuable for recurring benefits.

walkthrough.title: Outposts
walkthrough.text: Outposts are a special type of base with a shield icon. They act as a defensive wall. Your opponent cannot attack you directly or target your regular bases until all your Outposts are destroyed. This makes Outposts both defensive shields and priority targets. Combining Outposts with Authority-gaining effects can make you very hard to kill.

walkthrough.title: Faction Ally Abilities
walkthrough.text: Many cards have an ally ability that triggers when you have another card of the same faction in play during the same turn. For example, if you play two Trade Federation cards, both of their ally abilities activate. Bases in play from previous turns also count for ally triggers. This is why focusing on one or two factions is so powerful.

walkthrough.title: Scrap Abilities
walkthrough.text: Some cards have a scrap ability marked with a special icon. To use it, remove the card from the game permanently. The card goes to the scrap heap, not your discard pile, and you get a powerful one-time effect. Scrapping weak starting cards like Scouts and Vipers thins your deck and improves your average hand quality. You can scrap at any point during the Main Phase.

walkthrough.title: Deck Cycling
walkthrough.text: When your draw pile runs out, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw pile. This means every card you buy will eventually show up in your hand. The faster you cycle through your deck, the sooner you see your new powerful cards. Scrapping weak cards and having a lean deck means you draw your best cards more often.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when a player's Authority reaches zero or below. There is no final scoring or point tallying. The surviving player wins. Authority can go negative during a turn if multiple Combat hits land. Watch your opponent's Authority carefully because a big Combat turn can end the game out of nowhere.

Trade = buying power, Combat = attack opponent's Authority
Reduce opponent from 50 to 0 Authority to win

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Star Realms! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first few turns of a two-player game. You will learn how to play cards, buy from the Trade Row, attack your opponent, and build your deck. Both players start with fifty Authority and three-card opening hands. Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Player One: First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player one, play all three cards from your hand. You probably have two or three Scouts, which each give one Trade. Your pooled Trade might be two or three. Look at the Trade Row. Is there a card you can afford? Even a two-cost card is a great first purchase. If nothing looks good, buy an Explorer for two Trade. Every card you buy is stronger than your starting Scouts.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: First Turn
walkthrough.text: Player two, same process. Play your three cards and pool your Trade. If you drew a Viper, that gives one Combat instead of Trade. You can spend that Combat to deal one damage to Player one's Authority. Even one damage matters over a long game. Then spend your Trade on the best card you can afford from the Trade Row or grab an Explorer.

walkthrough.title: End of Turn: Draw Five
walkthrough.text: After your turn, discard all played cards and remaining hand cards to your discard pile. Then draw five cards from your personal deck. From now on, you will always draw five cards at the end of your turn. Your purchased cards are in your discard pile and will show up after you shuffle.

walkthrough.title: Choosing Your Faction Focus
walkthrough.text: As the Trade Row refreshes, look for faction patterns. If two blue Trade Federation cards appear, consider buying both. Having two cards of the same faction means their ally abilities will trigger when you draw them together. This is the core strategy of Star Realms: buy from one or two factions and let ally abilities amplify your power.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Buying from Every Faction
walkthrough.text: Player one sees a green card, then a yellow card, then a red card, and buys one of each because they all look cool. Three turns later, Player one draws all three but none of them trigger ally abilities because each faction appears only once. Meanwhile, Player two bought three green Blob cards and is triggering ally abilities every turn for bonus Combat and card draw.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Focus Your Purchases
walkthrough.text: No problem. From now on, Player one should prioritize buying cards from one or two factions. Look at what you already own and double down. If you have one blue card, look for more blue cards. Ally abilities are the biggest power multiplier in the game, and they only work with faction focus.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Base
walkthrough.text: Player two buys a base card. When played, it goes in front of you and stays there turn after turn. Each turn, you can use the base's ability again. Bases provide recurring value. If it is an Outpost, your opponent must destroy it before dealing damage to you. Bases are some of the best cards in the game because of this persistence.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Deck Thinning
walkthrough.text: After several turns, both players have bought new cards but neither has scrapped any starting cards. Their decks are now fifteen cards, but ten of them are still weak Scouts and Vipers. Hands are inconsistent. One turn you draw all Scouts, the next you draw all your good cards. The deck is bloated and unreliable.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Start Scrapping
walkthrough.text: Machine Cult cards and Explorers have scrap abilities that let you remove cards from the game. Use them to scrap your Scouts and Vipers. A ten-card deck with five good cards draws weak hands. An eight-card deck with five good cards draws great hands. Scrapping is not losing cards. It is concentrating your deck's power.

walkthrough.title: Dealing Damage
walkthrough.text: By now, both players should be generating meaningful Combat. Remember, unspent Combat is wasted at end of turn. Always attack. If your opponent has Outposts, you must destroy those first. Once all Outposts are down, pile your remaining Combat into your opponent's Authority. Chipping away at fifty Authority takes time, but big turns can deal ten or more damage.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Hoarding Combat
walkthrough.text: Player one has six Combat pooled but decides to save some for next turn. That is not how it works. Combat does not carry over between turns. At the end of your turn, any unspent Combat vanishes. Always spend all your Combat attacking before ending your turn.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Spend Everything
walkthrough.text: The fix is simple. Every turn, after playing all your cards and buying what you want, check your Combat pool. Spend every point of it on attacks. Hit Outposts first, then the opponent directly. There is never a reason to hold back Combat.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Power Spike
walkthrough.text: A few turns into the game, Player two draws a hand full of green Blob cards. Three ally abilities trigger, generating bonus Combat and card draw. The extra drawn cards provide even more Combat. Player two deals fourteen damage in a single turn. This is the Blob faction at its best: explosive burst damage powered by ally chains.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through the opening turns of Star Realms. You know how to buy cards, focus on factions, thin your deck, play bases, and attack your opponent. Remember: focus on one or two factions, scrap your weak cards, and never waste Combat. You are ready for a full game.

walkthrough.title: What to Explore Next
walkthrough.text:
Before your next battle, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on faction selection and deck building.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick descriptions of all four factions and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: What to Explore Next
bullets: Check out General Tips → faction selection and deck building
Reference the Appendix → faction descriptions and key terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Focus on One or Two Factions
walkthrough.text: The most important strategic principle in Star Realms is faction focus. Ally abilities are the primary power multiplier in the game, and they only trigger when you have multiple cards of the same faction in play. Buying from one main faction with a secondary splash faction gives you reliable ally triggers while maintaining flexibility. Spreading across all four factions means almost no ally abilities fire, leaving your cards at their weakest.

walkthrough.title: Thin Your Deck Early
walkthrough.text: Your starting deck has ten cards, and eight of them are Scouts that generate only one Trade each. These cards clog your draws and dilute your powerful purchases. Use Machine Cult scrap abilities or Explorer scrap abilities to remove Scouts and Vipers from the game. A leaner deck means you see your best cards more frequently, leading to stronger and more consistent turns.

walkthrough.title: Always Spend Your Combat
walkthrough.text: Combat does not carry over between turns. Any unspent Combat at the end of your turn disappears. This means you should always deal damage to your opponent or their bases. Even one point of damage matters over a thirty-turn game. Check your Combat pool before ending every turn and make sure it is at zero.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Bases
walkthrough.text: Bases provide recurring value every turn they survive. A base that grants two Trade each turn generates far more value over the game than a ship that gives three Trade once. Outposts are especially valuable because they shield you from direct damage. When choosing between a similarly priced ship and base, the base is usually the better investment.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Factions
walkthrough.text: Each faction has a distinct personality. Trade Federation is blue and specializes in Authority gain and Trade generation, making you durable. Blob is green and delivers high Combat output with card draw, enabling explosive burst turns. Star Empire is yellow and combines Combat with opponent discard effects, grinding your opponent's options. Machine Cult is red and excels at deck thinning through scrap abilities. Choose factions that match your preferred play style.

walkthrough.title: Balance Economy and Aggression
walkthrough.text: New players often go too heavy on Trade cards or too heavy on Combat cards. Pure Trade decks buy amazing cards but never deal damage. Pure Combat decks attack early but run out of fuel. The sweet spot is a mix where you can buy one good card per turn while still dealing meaningful damage. As the game progresses, shift toward more Combat since buying cards matters less when both decks are already strong.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Trade Row
walkthrough.text: The Trade Row is shared between both players. When a powerful card appears that perfectly fits your opponent's faction focus, consider buying it even if it does not help you. This is called hate-drafting, and it prevents your opponent from getting a key card. Conversely, if the row has nothing useful, buying a cheap card just to cycle the row can reveal something better.

walkthrough.title: Track Authority and Pace the Game
walkthrough.text: Always know both players' Authority totals. If you are ahead on Authority, you can afford to invest in economy and build a stronger deck. If you are behind, you need to shift toward immediate Combat to catch up. The game can end suddenly when a player draws their best hand and deals fifteen or more damage in a single turn. Stay aware of your opponent's potential burst damage.

4-faction spread = almost no ally bonuses fire

Leaner deck = see best cards more often

walkthrough.title: Blob Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Blob faction delivers the highest burst Combat in the game. A Blob rush strategy focuses on buying green cards with high Combat values and card draw ally abilities. When three or four Blob cards land in the same hand, ally abilities chain together for massive damage. The key is buying aggressively early, accepting weak mid-game turns in exchange for explosive late-game hands that can deal twenty or more Combat.

walkthrough.title: Trade Federation Durability
walkthrough.text: The Trade Federation strategy focuses on Authority gain and Trade generation. By buying blue cards that restore Authority, you extend the game and give yourself more turns to build an overwhelming deck. Combined with Outpost bases for defense, a Trade Federation build is very hard to kill. The downside is slower damage output, so pair it with a secondary Combat faction to maintain offensive pressure.

walkthrough.title: Machine Cult Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Machine Cult cards are the best deck thinners in the game. Their scrap abilities let you remove Scouts, Vipers, and even Explorers from your deck permanently. A Machine Cult strategy creates a tiny, hyper-efficient deck where every draw is a powerful card. The faction's Combat output is moderate, so pair it with Blob or Star Empire for kill potential while Machine Cult handles the deck quality.

walkthrough.title: Star Empire Control
walkthrough.text: Star Empire cards force your opponent to discard cards from their hand. This disruption prevents them from executing their plans while you build your own. Combined with decent Combat output, Star Empire can grind opponents down by denying them resources. The discard effects are especially punishing in the mid-game when opponents have invested in expensive cards that they never get to play.

walkthrough.title: Scrap Timing
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to scrap a card is one of the deepest decisions in Star Realms. Early game, keep your Explorers for their Trade value since you need the economy. Mid-game, scrap them for their two Combat when you would rather have damage. Some purchased cards have powerful scrap abilities that provide burst Combat or Authority gain. Hold these until a critical turn when the scrap effect can swing the game, then remove them for maximum impact.

walkthrough.title: Trade Row Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay as much attention to the Trade Row as they do to their own hand. If the row is filled with cards that benefit your opponent's faction, consider buying one just to deny it and cycle the row. If you see a powerful card you cannot afford this turn, buy a cheap card first to check if the replacement might be even better. Sometimes the best buy is not the most expensive card but the one that denies your opponent the most.

walkthrough.title: Lethal Calculation
walkthrough.text: In the late game, start calculating lethal. Count your opponent's Authority and estimate your maximum possible Combat on your next turn. Factor in ally abilities and scrap abilities you have not used yet. If you can threaten lethal, your opponent may be forced to play defensively, buying bases and Authority cards instead of advancing their own strategy. The threat of lethal is often as powerful as the lethal itself.

walkthrough.title: Adapting Your Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Trade Row is random, so you cannot always execute your preferred strategy. If Blob cards are not appearing, do not force a Blob build. Buy the best available cards from whatever faction is showing up. Flexibility is key because the player who adapts to the Trade Row wins more often than the player who waits for their ideal cards. Your strategy should be informed by what the row offers, not the other way around.

summary.title: Blob Rush Strategy
bullets: Green faction: highest burst Combat in the game
Ally abilities chain for massive damage (20+ Combat)
Accept weak mid-game for explosive late-game hands

summary.title: Trade Federation Durability
bullets: Blue faction: Authority gain + Trade generation
Extend the game → more turns to build overwhelming deck
Outposts for defense; pair with secondary Combat faction

summary.title: Machine Cult Efficiency
bullets: Red faction: best deck thinning via scrap abilities
Tiny, hyper-efficient deck where every draw is strong
Pair with Blob or Star Empire for kill potential

summary.title: Star Empire Control
bullets: Yellow faction: force opponent to discard cards
Disruption + decent Combat = grinding opponents down
Most punishing mid-game when opponents have invested heavily

summary.title: Scrap Timing
bullets: Early: keep Explorers for Trade value
Mid-game: scrap Explorers for 2 Combat
Hold powerful scrap abilities for critical swing turns

summary.title: Trade Row Manipulation
bullets: Buy opponent's key card to deny it (hate-draft)
Buy cheap cards to cycle row for better options
Best buy often = best denial, not highest cost

summary.title: Lethal Calculation
bullets: Count opponent's Authority vs your max Combat next turn
Factor in ally abilities + unused scrap abilities
Threat of lethal forces opponent into defensive plays

summary.title: Adapting Your Strategy
bullets: Trade Row is random — don't force a faction that isn't appearing
Buy best available cards regardless of original plan
Flexibility beats waiting for ideal cards

entries.definition: Each player's life total, starting at fifty. When a player's Authority reaches zero or below, they lose. Authority can be gained through Trade Federation cards and certain abilities.
The currency used to buy cards from the Trade Row and Explorer pile. Generated by playing cards with Trade values. Pooled during your turn and resets to zero at end of turn.
The resource used to deal damage to your opponent's Authority or destroy their bases. Pooled during your turn and resets to zero at end of turn. Unspent Combat is wasted.
The five face-up cards from the Trade Deck available for purchase. When a card is bought, immediately replace it from the Trade Deck.
The eighty-card face-down deck containing all ships and bases from four factions. Feeds the Trade Row as cards are purchased.
Ten Explorer cards always available for purchase at a cost of two Trade. Explorers provide two Trade and can be scrapped for two Combat.
The area where scrapped cards are placed. Cards in the scrap heap are permanently removed from the game and cannot return to any player's deck.
A card type that is played from hand, provides its effect during the Main Phase, and is discarded at the end of the turn. Ships do not persist between turns.
A card type that stays in play until destroyed by the opponent. Bases have a defense value and provide their ability each turn. More valuable than ships due to persistence.
A special type of base with a shield icon. Opponents must destroy all Outposts before they can deal Combat damage to the player or attack non-Outpost bases.
A starting deck card that provides one Trade. Scouts are weak and should be scrapped as soon as possible to thin your deck.
A starting deck card that provides one Combat. Like Scouts, Vipers are weak and benefit from being scrapped to improve deck quality.
A card available from the Explorer pile for two Trade. Provides two Trade when played and can be scrapped for two Combat. Good early economy that converts to damage later.
The blue faction specializing in Authority gain and Trade generation. Trade Federation builds are durable and hard to kill, extending the game for a stronger late-game deck.
The green faction specializing in high Combat output and card draw. Blob builds excel at burst damage turns where ally abilities chain for massive Combat.
The yellow faction combining Combat with opponent discard effects. Star Empire builds grind opponents down by denying them cards from their hand.
The red faction excelling at deck thinning through scrap abilities. Machine Cult builds create small, efficient decks where every draw is a powerful card.
The main ability of a card that resolves when the card is played (ships) or when it first enters play (bases).
A bonus ability that triggers when you have another card of the same faction in play during the same turn. Ally abilities are the primary reason to focus on one or two factions.
An optional one-time ability gained by permanently removing the card from the game. Scrap abilities provide powerful burst effects and are also used for deck thinning.
When your draw pile is empty, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw pile. Lean decks cycle faster, letting you see your best cards more often.
Buying a card from the Trade Row not because you need it but to prevent your opponent from getting it. A key defensive tactic when opponent's key faction card appears.
Some cards belong to two factions and can trigger ally abilities for either faction. These are especially versatile in multi-faction builds.
